questions.body: How many kinases belong to the serine/arginine kinase family of the human kinome?
questions.ideal_answer: The serine/arginine protein kinase (SRPK) family of the human kinome consists of three members: SRPK1, SRPK2, and SRPK3. SRPK1 and SRPK2 are well-established kinases that phosphorylate serine-arginine rich (SR) proteins to regulate pre-mRNA splicing, and both have been implicated in various cancers including melanoma, ovarian cancer, and nasopharyngeal carcinoma, as well as in hepatitis B virus capsid assembly. SRPK3 is less characterized but has been implicated in breast cancer, and selective chemical probes targeting SRPK3 have recently been developed to study its biological functions. All three family members share a conserved kinase domain and an SRPK-specific docking groove that mediates substrate recognition.

questions.body: What are the subtypes of Cushing Syndrome?
questions.ideal_answer: Cushing Syndrome subtypes are broadly divided into ACTH-dependent and ACTH-independent forms. ACTH-dependent CS includes Cushing's disease, caused by corticotroph pituitary tumors overproducing ACTH, and ectopic CS, resulting from nonpituitary neuroendocrine tumors that secrete ACTH. ACTH-independent CS encompasses adrenal CS, which involves autonomous cortisol production by adrenal adenomas, hyperplasia, or carcinomas. Additional recognized subtypes include functional non-neoplastic hypercortisolism, formerly known as pseudo-Cushing state, and mild autonomous cortisol secretion, often identified in the context of adrenal incidentalomas. These classifications guide differential diagnosis and inform management strategies, with surgical resection of the causative lesion remaining the first-line treatment across all subtypes.

questions.body: What are the symptoms of TAFRO syndrome?
questions.ideal_answer: TAFRO syndrome is a rare systemic inflammatory disorder whose name is an acronym derived from its cardinal symptoms. These include thrombocytopenia (low platelet count), anasarca (generalized fluid accumulation and edema), fever, reticulin fibrosis (fibrosis of bone marrow), renal dysfunction (kidney problems), and organomegaly (enlargement of organs such as the liver and spleen). The syndrome is associated with a cytokine storm and overlaps clinically with idiopathic multicentric Castleman disease. It can occur following infections such as COVID-19 or Epstein-Barr virus infection.

questions.body: What are the most common diseases caused by dysfunction of the ubiquitin system?
questions.ideal_answer: Dysfunction of the ubiquitin system is implicated in a range of serious human diseases. Cancer is linked to dysregulation of OTU deubiquitinases, which regulate proteasome-mediated protein degradation. Alzheimer's disease is associated with altered USP10 function affecting Tau pathology, as well as disrupted MLKL-USP7-UBA52 signaling that impairs ubiquitin homeostasis and autophagy in the brain. Parkinson's disease involves dysregulation of ubiquitin-mediated proteolysis pathways identified through transcriptomic and biomarker studies. Huntington's disease is connected to USP11 dysregulation affecting mutant huntingtin protein accumulation and mitochondrial dysfunction, as well as impaired SUMOylation and ubiquitination of misfolded huntingtin. Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis is associated with UBQLN2 dysfunction, a ubiquitin-proteasome system receptor, and with increased UFMylation in skeletal muscle. Together, these examples illustrate how perturbations in ubiquitin tagging, deubiquitinase activity, and related post-translational modification pathways contribute broadly to neurodegeneration and oncogenesis.

questions.body: What is the cortisol awakening response (CAR)?
questions.ideal_answer: The cortisol awakening response (CAR) is a rapid and robust increase in cortisol levels occurring within the first 30 to 45 minutes after morning awakening, representing a major component of the daily cortisol secretory pattern. In healthy individuals, this surge is regulated by an intricate dual-control system involving circadian, environmental, and neurocognitive processes, and is thought to serve important adaptive functions by preparing the organism for the challenges of the upcoming day. Specifically, the CAR is proposed to mobilize metabolic, immunological, and neurocognitive resources to meet activity-related demands, and to help counterregulate adverse emotional experiences from the prior day, acting through both rapid nongenomic and slower genomic pathways, including effects on circadian clock gene expression. Although a positive CAR—meaning cortisol rises after awakening—is the expected pattern, negative CAR values, where cortisol concentrations decrease after waking, are also observed and are not simply artifacts or errors. Research suggests that negative CAR values may reflect underlying health conditions or environmental exposures, such as elevated blood lead levels, and therefore carry potential biological and clinical relevance that warrants careful consideration rather than automatic exclusion from analyses.

questions.body: What  type of  blood cell has  CD19 as a surface biomarker?
questions.ideal_answer: CD19 is a surface biomarker found on B cells (B lymphocytes). It is a transmembrane glycoprotein, also known as the B-lymphocyte antigen CD19, that serves as a B cell-specific surface marker. CD19 is expressed on the majority of neoplastic B cells and has emerged as an attractive biomarker and therapeutic target for B-cell malignancies such as chronic lymphocytic leukaemia. Studies investigating peroxisome biogenesis in human peripheral blood mononuclear cells specifically identify CD19+ B cells as a distinct population, further confirming CD19 as a defining surface marker of this cell type.

questions.body: What are the most common vectors used in gene therapy?
questions.ideal_answer: The most common vectors used in gene therapy include both viral and non-viral systems. Among viral vectors, adeno-associated viruses (AAV) are currently the leading platform, accounting for the majority of recent FDA-approved gene therapies due to their safety profile and broad tropism. Lentiviral vectors are widely used for their ability to accommodate larger gene inserts and provide semipermanent expression, particularly in oncology and neurological applications. Adenoviruses and retroviruses have long been prevalent in clinical settings due to their high transduction efficiency. Herpes simplex virus (HSV) and baculoviral vectors are explored in cancer trials and cardiovascular applications, respectively. On the non-viral side, liposomes and lipid nanoparticles are increasingly important delivery systems, offering high nucleic acid loading capacity and reduced immunogenicity concerns compared to viral vectors, though they require continued optimization for in vivo safety and efficacy.

questions.body: What is the time to diagnosis for adrenal Cushing's Syndrome?
questions.ideal_answer: According to a meta-analysis based on 5367 patients assessing time to diagnosis in Cushing's Syndrome, the mean time to diagnosis for adrenal Cushing's Syndrome was 30 months. This was intermediate compared to ectopic CS, which had the shortest time to diagnosis at 14 months, and pituitary CS, which had the longest at 38 months. The overall mean time to diagnosis across all subtypes of CS was 34 months. Notably, no significant differences in time to diagnosis were found based on gender, age (under or over 18 years), or year of diagnosis (before or after 2000).

questions.body: What is the indication for Brineura?
questions.ideal_answer: Brineura (cerliponase alfa) is indicated to slow the loss of ambulation in pediatric patients with neuronal ceroid lipofuscinosis type 2 (CLN2). CLN2 is a pediatric neurodegenerative lysosomal storage disorder caused by biallelic mutations in the TPP1 gene, characterized by progressive motor function impairment, language deficiencies, seizures, ataxia, blindness, and early death. Cerliponase alfa is administered via intracerebroventricular infusion every two weeks and has been shown to reduce the progression of functional decline. It received its first global approval in the USA for the treatment of motor function loss in pediatric patients aged 3 years and older with CLN2, and subsequently received approval in the EU for CLN2 in patients of all ages.
exact_answer: to slow the loss of ambulation in pediatric patients with CLN2
treatment of motor function loss in paediatric patients ≥3 years of age with CLN2

questions.body: What is the primary energy source for cancer cells?
questions.ideal_answer: Glucose is the primary energy source for cancer cells. Multiple studies confirm that cancer cells undergo metabolic reprogramming characterized by preferential aerobic glycolysis, known as the Warburg effect, in which glucose is consumed at high rates even in the presence of oxygen to produce energy and lactate. Glucose transporters such as GLUT1 and GLUT5 are upregulated in various cancers to facilitate increased glucose uptake. While glucose is the dominant fuel, cancer cells also exhibit metabolic plasticity allowing them to utilize alternative substrates including glutamine (the second most utilized energy source after glucose), lactate (which can be recycled as an alternative fuel via monocarboxylate transporters), lipids (serving both as energy and signaling molecules), and fructose (metabolized through GLUT5 overexpression). Nevertheless, glucose remains the most frequently cited and best-supported primary energy source, underpinning the rapid proliferation, survival, invasion, and migration of cancer cells across multiple cancer types.

questions.body: Are adrenocortical secretory tumors in humans mostly a hereditary disorder?
questions.ideal_answer: Adrenocortical secretory tumors in humans are mostly sporadic, not hereditary. According to the available evidence, while these tumors can occasionally belong to hereditary syndromes that predispose individuals to tumor development, the majority of cases arise sporadically without an inherited genetic basis. The abstract explicitly states that such tumors are 'mostly sporadic but can belong to hereditary syndromes predisposing to tumors,' making clear that hereditary forms represent the minority rather than the majority of cases.

questions.body: Please describe alopecia areata.
questions.ideal_answer: Alopecia areata (AA) is an autoimmune disease characterized by nonscarring hair loss. Clinically, it presents as leukotrichia and/or alopecia without erythema, crusts, or excoriations, commonly affecting areas such as the face, dorsal cranium, and extremities. Histopathologically, AA is defined by bulbitis, with peribulbar cellular infiltrates primarily composed of lymphocytes, and often also including plasma cells, eosinophils, macrophages, and neutrophils. The condition can be associated with concurrent inflammatory conditions such as atopic dermatitis. Diagnosis is typically made clinically and supported by trichoscopy, though this technique requires specialized training; emerging deep learning models show promise in aiding both diagnosis and activity assessment, including distinguishing active, inactive, and regrowth phases. AA is considered a chronic, relapsing disease that frequently requires long-term treatment. Therapeutic options include immunosuppressive or immunomodulatory agents such as ciclosporin and oclacitinib, which can achieve partial or complete hair regrowth, though relapse is common upon dose reduction or discontinuation. Spontaneous remission can occur but is relatively uncommon. Overall, AA poses a significant management challenge due to its tendency to relapse and the need for ongoing therapy.

questions.body: What is the intracellular localization of PI4KIIalpha in human cells?
questions.ideal_answer: PI4KIIalpha localizes to multiple intracellular compartments in human and mammalian cells. Its primary localization is at the trans-Golgi network (TGN), where it colocalizes with TGN markers and contributes to PI4P synthesis. It is also found in the broader Golgi complex and juxtanuclear/perinuclear membrane pools. PI4KIIalpha is present on AP-3-derived vesicles and LAMP-1-positive endosomes, linking it to endosomal trafficking toward lysosomes. In neuronal cells, it is concentrated at synapses and on synaptic vesicles. In secretory cells such as RBL-2H3 mast cells, it localizes to exocytotic vesicles. Additionally, PI4KIIalpha associates with peripheral organelles and peripheral membranes, lipid raft fractions, and can be detected at the cellular surface and within other intracellular compartments. Its tight membrane association is conferred by palmitoylation of a CCPCC motif, which is critical for targeting to the TGN and lipid rafts.

questions.body: What is the prevalence of secondary adrenal insufficiency?
questions.ideal_answer: The prevalence of secondary adrenal insufficiency is estimated to be between 150 and 280 per million people. The most frequent cause is believed to be corticosteroid-induced insufficiency, although the clinical relevance of deficiency after cessation of glucocorticoid treatment remains widely debated. By comparison, primary adrenal insufficiency has a lower estimated prevalence of 82-144 per million. Both primary and secondary adrenal insufficiency are associated with acute adrenal crises occurring at approximately 6-8 per 100 patients per year in Europe, and alterations in quality of life in physical and mental health components have been demonstrated in both conditions.

questions.body: SynNeurGe classification system is associated with what disease?
questions.ideal_answer: The SynNeurGe classification system is associated with Parkinson's disease. It is a biologically based classification proposed to address the complexity and heterogeneity of Parkinson's disease by integrating three components: presence or absence of pathological α-synuclein (S) in tissues or CSF, evidence of neurodegeneration (N) defined by neuroimaging procedures, and documentation of pathogenic gene variants (G) that cause or strongly predispose to Parkinson's disease. These components are linked to a clinical component (C) defined by high-specificity or multiple lower-specificity clinical features. The system aims to facilitate both basic and clinical research and move the field toward precision medicine and disease-modifying therapies, with initial application recommended exclusively for research settings pending prospective validation.

questions.body: How is the pathogenic prion protein formed from the normal prion protein?
questions.ideal_answer: The pathogenic prion protein (PrPSc) is formed from the normal cellular prion protein (PrPC) through a conformational conversion process. In the favored model, existing PrPSc acts as a template, directly interacting with endogenous PrPC and inducing it to misfold into the pathogenic conformation, thereby propagating the infectious agent. This templating mechanism is supported by the observation that antibodies binding cell-surface PrPC can inhibit PrPSc formation in a dose-dependent manner, and that blocking the PrPC-PrPSc interaction prevents prion replication. Neurometals such as copper, zinc, iron, and manganese, which normally bind to PrPC, are critical determinants of this conformational change and can influence PrPC stability and toxicity. Specific point mutations also modulate susceptibility: the E200K mutation promotes pathogenic conversion by disrupting key salt-bridge interactions and increasing structural disorder between helices, while the E219K mutation confers greater stability. Additionally, certain agents like suramin or copper can induce PrPC misfolding at the plasma membrane, triggering internalization and degradation of aberrant conformers. Once PrPSc accumulates, cellular quality-control pathways involving proteins such as p62/SQSTM1 attempt to clear aggregates through autophagy and the proteasome system, though these mechanisms are often insufficient to halt disease progression.

questions.body: What is the average delay in the diagnosis of Cushing's Syndrome?
questions.ideal_answer: The average delay in the diagnosis of Cushing's Syndrome is approximately 34 months (about 2 years and 10 months) based on a meta-analysis of 5367 patients. This delay varies by subtype: ectopic CS has the shortest delay at 14 months, adrenal CS at 30 months, and pituitary CS the longest at 38 months. A large European registry (ERCUSYN) of 1564 patients reported a delay of approximately 2 years between symptom onset and diagnosis, with many specialists missing the correct diagnosis. A surgical cohort study reported a median time to diagnosis of 24 months. A patient survey found that 49% of CS patients experienced a diagnostic delay exceeding 2 years. No significant differences in delay were found based on gender, age, or year of diagnosis (before or after 2000). This prolonged diagnostic delay is largely attributed to the overlap of CS symptoms with common conditions such as metabolic syndrome, obesity, osteoporosis, and depression, underscoring the need for improved diagnostic strategies to reduce the burden of disease.

questions.body: Which of the 5 senses does otospongiosis affect?
questions.ideal_answer: Otospongiosis (also known as otosclerosis) affects the sense of hearing. It is a primary osteodystrophy of the otic capsule of the inner ear and one of the leading causes of deafness in adults. The condition causes fixation of the stapes footplate, resulting in conductive hearing loss; cochlear involvement can also produce sensorineural or mixed hearing loss. Conservative treatments such as sodium fluoride and bisphosphonates aim to stabilize hearing thresholds and slow disease progression, while surgical treatment (stapedotomy) is the definitive intervention, achieving closure of the air-bone gap and improvement in hearing outcomes.

questions.body: How is terminal differentiation regulated?
questions.ideal_answer: Terminal differentiation is regulated through a coordinated interplay of transcriptional, epigenetic, and cell cycle mechanisms that enforce permanent cell cycle exit. Master transcription factors such as MyoD, NGN2, SOX11, and Ascl1 drive lineage specification and initiate withdrawal from the cell cycle, often through a rapid molecular switch operating exclusively in G1 that simultaneously triggers differentiation commitment and permanent cell cycle exit. Epigenetically, this process involves heterochromatin clustering and progressive reduction in chromatin accessibility at enhancers of key cell cycle regulators including Cyclin E, E2F1, and String, acting as a cell cycle-independent timer that limits responses to mitogenic signals. The retinoblastoma protein (RB) maintains the postmitotic state by repressing E2F transcription factors, while the DREAM complex suppresses cell cycle gene expression. Notably, heterochromatin clustering appears to be a consequence of cell cycle exit rather than a driver of differentiation per se. The cilium-centrosome axis further influences the choice between reversible quiescence and irreversible terminal differentiation. Although typically permanent, terminally differentiated cells retain latent proliferative potential, as demonstrated by adenoviral E1A-mediated cell cycle reactivation, highlighting that the postmitotic state requires active, ongoing molecular enforcement rather than simple loss of proliferative machinery.

questions.body: What are the common symptoms of adrenal insufficiency?
questions.ideal_answer: Common symptoms of adrenal insufficiency include general weakness, fatigue, anorexia, arthralgia, fever, nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, myalgias, weight loss, and salt craving. Laboratory findings frequently reveal hyponatremia and hyperkalemia. Without treatment, patients often develop hyperpigmented skin and mucus membranes. In acute adrenal crisis, which is a medical emergency, symptoms can escalate to include hypotension, lethargy, tachycardia, and hypoglycemia. Symptoms typically develop slowly over several months and are related to alterations in salt and water balance caused by deficient production of mineralocorticoids, glucocorticoids, and adrenal androgens.

questions.body: Krabbe disease, also known as globoid-cell leukodystrophy is thought to results from what toxic metabolite?
questions.ideal_answer: Krabbe disease, also known as globoid cell leukodystrophy, is caused by a functional deficiency of the lysosomal enzyme galactocerebrosidase (GALC), which leads to the progressive accumulation of the toxic metabolite psychosine. Psychosine accumulation affects many different cellular pathways, leading to severe demyelination of both the central and peripheral nervous systems. Additionally, psychosine has been shown to potentiate inflammatory responses, including increased TNF-alpha production by peripheral blood mononuclear cells in Krabbe disease patients, further contributing to disease pathogenesis.

questions.body: How do cancer cells attack the cells of the immune system?
questions.ideal_answer: Cancer cells employ multiple strategies to attack and evade immune cells, particularly T cells. They can upregulate the PD-L1/PD-1 axis, whereby PD-L1 expressed on cancer cells binds PD-1 on T cells, inducing T-cell apoptosis or anergy. Cancer cells also promote M2 polarization of tumor-associated macrophages (TAMs) through pathways such as the ZEB1-MCSF-CCL18 axis or via extracellular vesicles carrying NEAT1, which ultimately drives CD8+ T-cell apoptosis. Proteins like SPOCK1 suppress CD8+ T-cell viability and promote immune evasion in lung cancer. In hepatocellular carcinoma, high cathepsin E (CTSE) expression drives release of des-gamma-carboxy prothrombin (DCP), which increases reactive oxygen species in T cells and causes their apoptosis. Extracellular galectin 4 secreted by pancreatic cancer cells binds CD3 on T cells, directly inducing their apoptosis. Cancer-derived exosomes can deliver microRNAs such as miR-24-3p into T cells, impairing T-cell survival and promoting regulatory T-cell development. Additionally, factors in cancer patient serum promote regulatory T-cell differentiation and T-cell exhaustion through cytokines like IL-4, IL-10, and TGF-beta. Together, these mechanisms create an immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment that protects cancer cells from immune destruction.

questions.body: When and how does an Addisonian crisis manifest?
questions.ideal_answer: An Addisonian crisis, or acute adrenal crisis, is a life-threatening medical emergency that occurs when the body's demand for cortisol is not met, typically triggered by acute illness, gastrointestinal or other infection, fever, trauma, surgery, or significant physical or psychological stress in a patient with underlying adrenal insufficiency. It can also be precipitated by inadequate glucocorticoid supplementation or abrupt discontinuation of steroid therapy. Symptoms can develop within hours and include severe hypotension and hypovolemic shock that may be unresponsive to fluids or vasopressors, tachycardia, profound weakness, nausea and vomiting, abdominal pain, muscle and joint pain, drowsiness, altered mental status, hyponatremia, and hypoglycemia. In patients with primary adrenal insufficiency, hyperpigmentation and signs of chronic adrenal insufficiency such as salt craving may also be present. If the crisis is overlooked, diagnosed late, or treated with insufficient doses or by an inappropriate route, there is a considerable risk of death. Immediate treatment consists of intravenous or intramuscular hydrocortisone (100 mg bolus) followed by continuous infusion, along with intravenous fluid resuscitation and blood pressure support, until the precipitating cause resolves and oral replacement can be resumed.

questions.body: Is Calcium dyshomeostasis  assoiciated with Huntington's disease?
questions.ideal_answer: Yes, calcium dyshomeostasis is associated with Huntington's disease. Multiple lines of evidence support this connection. Huntington's disease is caused by an expanded CAG repeat in the huntingtin gene, and the resulting mutant protein disrupts neuronal calcium homeostasis, particularly in striatal neurons. Research shows a positive feedback loop between calcium dyshomeostasis and huntingtin protein aggregation, which escalates over time and contributes to neuronal loss. Mitochondrial calcium dyshomeostasis is specifically implicated, as mitochondria play a key role in buffering intracellular calcium, and their dysfunction in Huntington's disease further impairs calcium regulation. Additionally, endoplasmic reticulum dysfunction, impaired calcium buffering, glutamate excitotoxicity, and alterations in calcium entry routes into neurons all contribute to the overall calcium dyshomeostasis observed in the disease. These disturbances in calcium homeostasis are considered important factors linking the huntingtin mutation to the onset and progression of Huntington's disease, making calcium signaling pathways a significant area of interest for therapeutic strategies targeting this disorder.

questions.body: What are the most common mechanisms leading to amyloid formation?
questions.ideal_answer: Amyloid formation arises through several interconnected mechanisms. At the molecular level, charge-charge interactions and the salting-out effect (Hofmeister series) can accelerate aggregation, while molecules like ATP may disrupt the supersaturated state above thermodynamic solubility to trigger fibril formation. Conformational transitions, such as shifts to a molten globule-like intermediate state, prime proteins for aggregation. Protein misfolding and aggregation are central processes, with nucleation-driven fibril growth being a hallmark of ordered amyloid assembly. Membrane protein dimerization, as seen with the amyloid precursor protein APP, influences amyloid peptide production and subsequent aggregation into fibrils and oligomers. Genetic mutations and single nucleotide polymorphisms can predispose specific proteins to amyloid formation. At the systemic level, an imbalance between in vivo amyloid production and natural clearance mechanisms drives disease progression. Environmental factors such as metabolic stress can also promote amyloid deposition, as observed with IAPP in pancreatic beta cells. Together, these mechanisms spanning molecular interactions, protein conformational dynamics, genetic predisposition, and homeostatic imbalance underpin amyloid formation across a wide range of diseases.
exact_answer: disrupting the supersaturated state above the thermodynamic solubility
imbalance between amyloid production and natural clearance
conformational transition to molten globule-like state

questions.body: How is acute adrenal crisis treated?
questions.ideal_answer: Acute adrenal crisis is a life-threatening medical emergency requiring immediate intervention. The cornerstone of treatment is prompt administration of high-dose parenteral glucocorticoids, typically intravenous hydrocortisone at 10–15 times physiological doses. If intravenous access cannot be rapidly established, intramuscular hydrocortisone should be given without delay; prednisolone or methylprednisolone are acceptable alternatives if hydrocortisone is unavailable. Alongside glucocorticoid replacement, aggressive intravenous fluid resuscitation is essential to address hypotension and dehydration, and electrolyte abnormalities such as hyponatremia and hyperkalemia, as well as hypoglycemia, must be identified and corrected. Blood pressure support may also be required. Prevention and long-term management center on patient education: individuals with adrenal insufficiency should be instructed on stress dosing—increasing glucocorticoid doses during illness, trauma, surgery, or other physical stress—and trained in self-administering injectable glucocorticoids. Patients should carry a steroid dependency alert card and wear a medical alert bracelet. However, studies show that nearly half of patients are unable to self-inject during a crisis due to illness-related confusion and weakness, highlighting the importance of caregiver training, engagement with emergency medical services, and development of easier-to-use delivery devices such as autoinjectors.

questions.body: is Paracoccidioidomycosis caused by a parasite?
questions.ideal_answer: Paracoccidioidomycosis is not caused by a parasite. It is a systemic mycosis, meaning it is a fungal disease. The causative agent is Paracoccidioides brasiliensis and related species (Paracoccidioides spp.), which are dimorphic fungi endemic to Latin America. Multiple abstracts explicitly describe the disease as a fungal infection: Abstract 1 calls it a neglected tropical fungal disease, Abstract 2 describes growth of Paracoccidioides brasiliensis on culture with yeast observed on pathology, and Abstract 3 identifies the causative organism as the dimorphic fungus Paracoccidioides brasiliensis. Fungi and parasites are distinct biological categories, and Paracoccidioides belongs firmly to the kingdom Fungi, not to the parasitic organisms such as protozoa or helminths.

questions.body: What are the most common bacteria in the human skin microbiome?
questions.ideal_answer: The most common bacteria in the human skin microbiome span several genera and species. Staphylococcus is a dominant genus, with Staphylococcus epidermidis being the most ubiquitous skin commensal. Streptococcus species, including S. mitis and S. parasanguinis, are also found in high relative abundance. Corynebacterium, particularly Corynebacterium tuberculostearicum, represents another major component of the skin microbiome and is among the most prevalent genera identified across body sites. Cutibacterium, especially Cutibacterium acnes, is a predominant member particularly on sebaceous skin areas such as the face. Micrococcus and Kocuria are also recognized as core skin microbiome genera. The Neisseriaceae family has been identified as a predominant part of the facial skin microbiome in some studies. Together, these taxa form the core bacterial community of healthy human skin, with their relative abundances varying by body site, individual, and environmental factors.

questions.body: What is the mean time to diagnosis for ectopic Cushing's Syndrome?
questions.ideal_answer: According to a meta-analysis based on 5367 patients, the mean time to diagnosis for ectopic Cushing's Syndrome is 14 months. This is notably shorter than the other subtypes: adrenal Cushing's Syndrome has a mean time to diagnosis of 30 months, and pituitary Cushing's Syndrome has the longest at 38 months. The overall mean time to diagnosis across all subtypes of Cushing's Syndrome is 34 months. No significant differences in time to diagnosis were found based on gender, age (under or over 18 years), or year of diagnosis (before or after 2000).

questions.body: What is the realtionship between survivin and nasopharyngeal cancer?
questions.ideal_answer: Survivin is significantly overexpressed in nasopharyngeal carcinoma (NPC) tissues compared to normal nasopharyngeal or chronic nasopharyngitis tissues, and this elevated expression is strongly associated with poor patient prognosis. Meta-analysis of 26 studies encompassing over 2,000 patients confirmed that high survivin expression correlates with reduced overall survival, increased lymph node metastasis, local recurrence, distant metastasis, and higher clinical staging. Survivin functions as an independent risk factor for unfavorable outcomes in NPC and is considered a valuable biomarker for predicting disease malignancy and prognosis. Its expression is positively correlated with that of β-catenin and TCF-4, suggesting involvement of the Wnt signaling pathway in NPC progression. Mechanistically, survivin overexpression promotes tumor cell proliferation, and experimental suppression of survivin via shRNA in NPC cell lines distinctly inhibits proliferation, highlighting its functional role in disease onset and development. Beyond prognosis, survivin also shows diagnostic utility: autoantibodies against survivin are elevated in NPC patients, and a panel combining survivin autoantibodies with Cyclin B1, NY-ESO-1, and IMP3 achieves an AUC of 0.779 for NPC detection, suggesting potential as an early diagnostic tool.

questions.body: Is the inactive X chromosome (Barr body) less susceptible to genotoxic damage or mutations than the active X chromosome?
questions.ideal_answer: The inactive X chromosome (Barr body) is not less susceptible to genotoxic damage or mutations than the active X chromosome. Studies using X-irradiation of human lymphocytes show that both the active and inactive X chromosomes exhibit similar radiosensitivity, with a comparable number of breaks induced in each (Abstract 8). While repair of UV-induced damage is slower in inactive X-chromosomal genes compared to active housekeeping genes (Abstracts 6 and 7), this reflects differences in repair efficiency rather than reduced susceptibility to initial damage. In fact, repair inhibition studies suggest the inactive X chromosome may be more vulnerable to the consequences of unrepaired breaks, as aberrations involving it increase more after repair inhibition (Abstract 8). Additionally, DNA damage from dysfunctional telomeres disrupts the epigenetic stability of the inactive X chromosome, causing reactivation of silenced genes (Abstract 4). The conserved DNA damage response at the Xi, including chromatin relaxation and relocation of damage sites, confirms it participates fully in damage responses (Abstract 3). Thus, the heterochromatic state of the Xi does not protect it from genotoxic insult; if anything, slower repair may make it more prone to accumulating mutations.

questions.body: What are the causes of the different categories of adrenal insufficiency?
questions.ideal_answer: Adrenal insufficiency, defined by inadequate cortisol production, is categorized into three forms based on etiology. Primary adrenal insufficiency results from direct failure of the adrenal gland itself, most commonly due to autoimmune destruction (as in Addison's disease), but also caused by congenital adrenal hyperplasia, infections such as tuberculosis or fungal diseases, pharmacological inhibition (e.g., high-dose azole antifungals), or surgical removal of adrenal cortical tissue. This form involves deficiency of all adrenocortical hormones, including mineralocorticoids and androgens in addition to glucocorticoids. Secondary adrenal insufficiency arises from hypothalamic-pituitary pathologies that reduce ACTH stimulation of the adrenal glands, with causes including pituitary tumors, hemorrhage, inflammatory or infiltrative conditions such as hypophysitis, sarcoidosis, or hemochromatosis, as well as surgery, radiation therapy, or medications that suppress corticotropin production such as opioids. Glucocorticoid-induced adrenal insufficiency, the most common form, results from exogenous administration of supraphysiological doses of glucocorticoids, which suppress the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis; it is typically suspected when patients taper or discontinue such therapy. Each category has distinct hormonal profiles and requires tailored diagnostic and management approaches.

questions.body: Please list the common side effects of Kymriah
questions.ideal_answer: Based on the provided context, the common side effects associated with Kymriah (tisagenlecleucel), an anti-CD19 CAR T-cell therapy, include cytokine release syndrome (CRS) and CAR T-cell-related encephalopathy syndrome (CRES). These toxicities can be severe or even fatal, and their careful management is considered vital in patients undergoing this treatment for relapsed or refractory B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia.

questions.body: Which factors trigger eustress responses in the body?
questions.ideal_answer: Eustress responses in the body are triggered by a range of factors. Regular physical exercise generates reactive oxygen species (ROS) that modulate redox-sensitive signaling pathways, promoting adaptive responses in skeletal muscle and beyond. Environmental enrichment, which provides enhanced sensory, cognitive, motor, and social stimulation, functions as a hormetic stimulus that induces neuroplasticity, neurotrophic factor expression, and stress resilience. Specific phytochemicals such as luteolin activate the Nrf2-Keap1 pathway, contributing to oxidative eustress and redox balance. Preconditioning with small doses of stressful stimuli and mild pro-oxidant activity mobilizes antioxidant defense mechanisms, supporting optimal redox homeostasis. In humans, analogous enrichments including physical exercise, cognitive challenges, social engagement, and music facilitate neuroplasticity and promote stress resilience through hormetic biphasic dose-response mechanisms, where moderate stimulation produces beneficial outcomes that can become detrimental with excessive exposure.

questions.body: Is Cushing Syndrome present in canine populations?
questions.ideal_answer: Yes, Cushing's syndrome is present in canine populations. It is described as a common endocrine disorder in dogs, also known as naturally occurring hypercortisolism. Epidemiological data from UK primary-care veterinary practices estimate a 1-year period prevalence of 0.17% and an annual incidence of 0.06% among dogs under veterinary care. The condition is caused primarily by ACTH-producing pituitary adenomas (pituitary-dependent hypercortisolism, accounting for 80-85% of cases) or by adrenocortical tumors (15-20% of cases). Certain breeds are at higher risk, including Bichon frises, Border terriers, and Miniature schnauzers, while Golden retrievers and Labrador retrievers appear more protected. Increasing age and bodyweight above the breed-sex mean are also associated with higher odds of diagnosis. Machine learning tools have been developed to assist in its diagnosis, and multiple treatment options exist, including surgical intervention, radiotherapy, and pharmacotherapy with agents such as trilostane or mitotane.

questions.body: Does a zoonosis affect humans?
questions.ideal_answer: Yes, zoonoses affect humans. A zoonosis is by definition a disease transmissible from animals to humans. The abstracts provide multiple concrete examples. Western equine encephalitis is described as a zoonotic disease in which humans are accidental hosts, capable of causing meningoencephalitis, particularly in children. Spotted fever group rickettsioses caused by Rickettsia species pose a significant zoonotic threat to public health. Tularemia is a nationally notifiable zoonosis in the United States that caused 2,462 reported human cases between 2011 and 2022, with a case fatality rate that can exceed 2% depending on the strain. Crimean-Congo hemorrhagic fever is a tick-borne zoonotic disease present across many African countries, with serologic evidence of human exposure documented in northern Tanzania, linked to livestock-rearing activities. Together, these examples confirm that zoonotic diseases can and do affect humans, sometimes causing severe illness and death.

questions.body: How do larval salivary glands differ from adult salivary glands in Drosophila melanogaster?
questions.ideal_answer: The larval salivary gland in Drosophila melanogaster is a simple epithelial tubular organ specialized for high-level protein synthesis and secretion. It functions both as an exocrine organ, secreting glue proteins that attach the forming pupa to a solid surface, and as an endocrine organ, releasing the peptide Sgsf into the hemolymph to regulate systemic growth by modulating insulin-like peptide secretion and mTOR signaling. In contrast, adult salivary glands are derived from imaginal tissues and persist after metamorphosis. The larval glands are not retained during the larval-to-adult transition; instead, they undergo programmed cell death involving growth arrest, autophagy, crinophagy of excess secretory granules, and caspase-dependent apoptosis. This degradation is triggered by the steroid hormone 20-hydroxyecdysone, which induces the transcription factor E93 to promote expression of pro-apoptotic RHG genes (reaper, hid, and grim). The gene l(2)gl also influences the timing of histolysis in a dose-dependent manner. Cytoskeletal rearrangements in microtubule and actin networks accompany these cell death processes. Thus, larval salivary glands are transient, multifunctional secretory organs that are systematically eliminated at metamorphosis, whereas adult salivary glands are stable structures that differentiate from imaginal precursors.

questions.body: How often is leukocytosis present in patients with Cushing's Syndrome?
questions.ideal_answer: Leukocytosis is a frequent finding in patients with Cushing's Syndrome, though its reported prevalence varies by study. A smaller study of 26 patients with Cushing's disease found elevated white blood cell counts in approximately 40% of cases (11/28 events), with mean baseline WBC of 10,500 cells/µl dropping significantly after treatment. A larger nationwide retrospective cohort study of 297 patients with Cushing's Syndrome reported leukocytosis in 21.5% of patients at baseline, compared to 8.9% in matched controls. Both studies confirmed significantly higher WBC counts, neutrophil counts, and neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratios in Cushing's Syndrome patients compared to controls, reflecting direct glucocorticoid effects on white blood cells. Post-surgical remission led to significant decreases in WBC and neutrophil counts, though levels did not fully normalize in all patients. Notably, baseline leukocytosis was associated with a lower remission rate in Cushing's disease patients (36.7% vs. 63.9% in those with normal WBC).

questions.body: What are the hallmarks of Acro-cardio-facial syndrome (ACFS)
questions.ideal_answer: Acro-cardio-facial syndrome (ACFS) is a rare genetic disorder with a broad constellation of congenital anomalies. The core hallmarks include split-hand/split-foot malformation (ectrodactyly), cleft lip/palate, congenital heart defects, facial anomalies, and genital anomalies. Intellectual disability (mental retardation) and growth retardation are also consistent features. Additional findings that may expand the phenotypic spectrum include mild scoliosis and hemivertebrae. An autosomal recessive inheritance pattern has been suggested based on parental consanguinity and recurrence in siblings, though some evidence points to a microdeletion of chromosome 6q21-q22.3 as a possible cause in certain cases. Prognosis is generally poor, with many affected individuals not surviving the first days or months of life.

questions.body: What are the effects of D-amino acids on human health?
questions.ideal_answer: D-amino acids exert wide-ranging effects on human health across neurological, metabolic, oncological, renal, and infectious disease domains. In the brain, D-serine, D-aspartate, and D-alanine interact with NMDA and metabotropic glutamate receptors, and their reduced levels are associated with schizophrenia and neurodegenerative diseases such as Alzheimer's disease, making them both pathophysiological contributors and potential therapeutic targets. D-amino acids also serve as promising biomarkers for chronic kidney disease, malignancy, and infectious diseases, offering more accurate and less invasive diagnostic approaches than conventional markers like creatinine. From dietary sources, D-amino acids accumulate in tissues and influence lipid metabolism, glycerophospholipid pathways, and neurotransmission through choline metabolism. In oncology, D-amino acid oxidase combined with D-amino acid supplementation suppresses hepatocellular carcinoma growth and can reverse sorafenib resistance. D-amino acids also demonstrate antimicrobial and antibiofilm properties against pathogens such as Pseudomonas aeruginosa, particularly relevant in cystic fibrosis-related lung infections, and show promise as natural food preservatives due to their activity against foodborne pathogens. Collectively, these findings highlight D-amino acids as multifunctional molecules with significant therapeutic and diagnostic potential across diverse areas of human health.

questions.body: What is the mean time to diagnosis for pituitary Chushing's Syndrome?
questions.ideal_answer: Based on a meta-analysis of 5367 patients with Cushing's syndrome, the mean time to diagnosis for pituitary Cushing's syndrome (Cushing's disease) is 38 months. This is longer than the overall mean time to diagnosis for all Cushing's syndrome subtypes combined, which is 34 months. Among the subtypes, ectopic Cushing's syndrome had the shortest time to diagnosis at 14 months, adrenal Cushing's syndrome at 30 months, and pituitary Cushing's syndrome at 38 months, with statistically significant differences between subtypes (P < .001). Notably, patients with pituitary Cushing's syndrome in Germany had an even longer time to diagnosis compared to other countries. No significant differences in time to diagnosis were found based on gender, age (under or over 18 years), or year of diagnosis (before or after 2000). The prolonged diagnostic delay is attributed to the overlap of Cushing's syndrome signs and symptoms with common conditions such as metabolic syndrome, obesity, osteoporosis, and depression.

questions.body: Has Vitamin D therapy been shown to improve cognitive function in Alzheimers disease?
questions.ideal_answer: While observational studies suggest an association between higher vitamin D levels and improved cognition, and lower vitamin D levels have been linked to Alzheimer's disease, more rigorous Mendelian Randomization analyses found no clear causal evidence that vitamin D supplementation improves cognitive function in Alzheimer's disease. The MR study, designed to reduce confounding bias, showed null effects of genetically estimated vitamin D levels on cognitive performance measures including PACC scores in the general population and in those over 60. Cross-sectional data confirm that AD patients have lower vitamin D levels compared to those without dementia, but this association does not establish that therapy improves cognition. Mechanistic and experimental studies suggest vitamin D may have neuroprotective and anti-inflammatory roles through various cellular pathways, and some reviews propose calcitriol as a potentially beneficial supplement, but these remain theoretical. Overall, the current evidence does not support a demonstrated improvement in cognitive function from vitamin D therapy in Alzheimer's disease, and further rigorous clinical trials are needed before such a conclusion can be drawn.

questions.body: Can AlphaFold be used in a reverse manner to predict a possible amino acid sequence from a desired protein structure (inverse protein folding)?
questions.ideal_answer: Yes, AlphaFold can be used in a reverse manner to predict amino acid sequences from a desired protein structure, a task known as inverse protein folding. While AlphaFold itself is a forward-folding model that predicts structure from sequence, its capabilities have been harnessed to support inverse folding in several ways. One approach uses knowledge distillation of AlphaFold's confidence metrics (such as pTM or pLDDT scores) to create faster, end-to-end differentiable models that serve as structural consistency regularizers during inverse folding model training, yielding improvements in sequence recovery and protein diversity. Tools like SeqPredNN predict amino acid sequences expected to fold into specified structures and then use AlphaFold to validate the predicted folds, achieving a median TM-score of 0.638 against crystal structures. More broadly, the field of inverse protein folding has advanced significantly alongside AlphaFold, with methods like ProRefiner and physics-informed neural networks also tackling the problem of designing sequences compatible with given backbone structures. Thus, while AlphaFold is not natively an inverse folding tool, it plays a central role in enabling and validating inverse protein folding approaches.

questions.body: Is Cushing syndrome present in feline populations?
questions.ideal_answer: Yes, Cushing's syndrome (hyperadrenocorticism) is present in feline populations. Although it is diagnosed far less frequently in cats than in dogs, its incidence in cats is thought to be similar to that in humans. Feline hyperadrenocorticism has distinct clinical features, including unregulated diabetes mellitus and severe skin atrophy, which are unique to cats. Other signs shared with dogs include a potbellied appearance, polydipsia, polyuria, and increased susceptibility to infections. Clinicians must be aware that diagnostic tests for feline hyperadrenocorticism differ from those used in dogs, and treatment can be challenging. Overall, feline adrenal disorders, including Cushing's syndrome, are becoming increasingly recognized in veterinary medicine.

questions.body: Is Heck's disease caused by a bacterium?
questions.ideal_answer: Heck's disease is not caused by a bacterium. It is caused by the human papillomavirus (HPV), a virus. Also known as focal epithelial hyperplasia (FEH), the condition involves HPV-induced proliferation of epithelial cells that produces asymptomatic lesions in the oral mucosa. It is more prevalent among children and within families, and while the lesions are generally self-limiting, they can progress or cause aesthetic and functional discomfort, making early diagnosis important.

questions.body: Is situs inversus totalis compatible with life?
questions.ideal_answer: Yes, situs inversus totalis (SIT) is compatible with life. Multiple case reports and reviews confirm that patients with SIT do not have a decreased survival rate compared to the general population, as the condition generally carries no pathophysiological significance on its own. It is a rare congenital anomaly occurring in approximately 1 in 8,000 to 1 in 25,000 live births, inherited in an autosomal recessive pattern, and is often discovered incidentally when patients seek care for unrelated medical issues. Patients can live normal lives, as illustrated by cases including a 93-year-old woman and elderly patients undergoing cancer surgery. The main concerns arise when SIT is associated with other conditions such as primary ciliary dyskinesia, which can lead to respiratory complications, or when patients require surgical intervention, as the mirror-image anatomy can challenge clinical diagnosis and operative planning. Nonetheless, with proper radiological evaluation and careful surgical preparation, patients with SIT can safely undergo procedures ranging from gastrostomy to colectomy. Clinicians are advised to be aware of this condition and counsel affected patients that it is compatible with a normal lifespan.

questions.body: What is the treatment of choice for Cushing's Disease?
questions.ideal_answer: The treatment of choice for Cushing's disease is trans-sphenoidal surgery (TSS). While TSS is the preferred initial treatment, it is not always curative and carries a notable risk of recurrence, with published recurrence rates varying between 5 and 20%. Approximately half of recurrences appear within 5 years post-surgery and the remaining half within or even after 10 years. A low or undetectable cortisol level in the immediate post-operative period is regarded as the best criterion of remission, though it does not accurately predict long-term outcomes. Current guidelines recommend that every patient in remission be monitored for possible recurrence using methods such as late night salivary cortisol, dexamethasone suppression testing, and urinary free cortisol measurement.

questions.body: Why do men die earlier than women?
questions.ideal_answer: Men generally die earlier than women, a pattern observed globally across many sexually reproducing species. Multiple biological factors contribute to this disparity. Men exhibit faster telomere shortening and shorter telomere lengths than women, even from birth, which is associated with accelerated biological aging, increased arterial stiffness, and greater cardiovascular risk. Sex steroids play an important role; testosterone, a key driver of male reproductive success, is associated with poorer survival, while estrogens may confer protective effects in women. Metabolic differences also contribute, as females show greater plasticity in response to dietary restriction and nutrient-sensing pathways, and sex-specific genes such as UGT2B11 regulate lipid metabolism in ways that favor female longevity. At the population level, men experience higher rates of external mortality in younger adulthood and greater smoking-related and cardiovascular disease mortality in older age. Men also display greater lifespan variation, reflecting broader inequalities in length of life. Together, these findings suggest that the male-female longevity gap arises from a complex interplay of genetic, hormonal, cellular, and behavioral factors, and that understanding the cross-regulation of steroid hormone and nutrient-sensing pathways may be especially important for explaining sex differences in aging and lifespan.

questions.body: What is the difference between Cushing Syndrome and Cushing Disease?
questions.ideal_answer: Cushing's syndrome refers to the general condition of sustained pathologic hypercortisolism, which can arise from multiple causes: excessive ACTH secretion by pituitary tumors (Cushing's disease, ~70% of cases), ectopic ACTH secretion from tumors elsewhere (~15%), or ACTH-independent cortisol secretion from adrenal tumors (~15%). Cushing's disease is therefore a specific subtype of Cushing's syndrome in which the hypercortisolism is driven by a pituitary tumor producing excess ACTH. In clinical practice, differentiating a pituitary source of excess ACTH (Cushing's disease) from an ectopic source is accomplished by imaging the pituitary and sampling ACTH in the venous drainage of the pituitary (bilateral inferior petrosal sinus sampling). The prognosis is better for Cushing's disease and benign adrenal causes of Cushing's syndrome than for adrenocortical cancer and malignant ACTH-producing tumors.
exact_answer: Cushing's disease is a specific subtype of Cushing's syndrome caused by a pituitary tumor secreting excess ACTH
Cushing's syndrome is the general condition of sustained pathologic hypercortisolism from any cause
Cushing's disease accounts for approximately 70% of Cushing's syndrome cases
